content.text: The Core Legal Problem: Who Owns What an AI Creates?

When a software developer asks a code-generation model to write a function, a marketing analyst asks a language model to draft a campaign brief, or a design team uses a diffusion model to generate product images, the enterprise faces a legal question that copyright law did not contemplate: if a human author is required for copyright to vest, and the AI did the creative work, does the enterprise own anything?

This question has material business consequences. An output with no copyright protection cannot be exclusively owned — competitors can copy it without infringement. An output that carries copyright but owned by the AI vendor, not the enterprise, means the enterprise may be licensing rather than owning a key business asset. And an output derived from third-party copyrighted training data may carry infringement risk that the enterprise unknowingly inherits.

Copyright law in every jurisdiction was designed for human authors. Its application to AI-generated content is being actively litigated and legislated. The frameworks below reflect the law as of mid-2024 — enterprises must monitor this space and re-assess annually.
The Decision Framework

The decision framework above identifies four outcomes depending on the degree of human creative contribution and the operating jurisdiction. The critical question is not whether a human was involved — pressing a button counts as involvement — but whether the human made
sufficiently creative choices
that copyright law would recognise as authorship.
Jurisdictional Positions
United States

The US Copyright Office has consistently held that copyright requires human authorship. In the Thaler v. Perlmutter case (2023), the DC Circuit affirmed that a work created entirely by an AI system without human creative control is not copyrightable. The Copyright Office's 2023 guidance on AI further clarified:
Purely AI-generated outputs — where a human provides only a prompt and accepts the output — receive no copyright protection.
Human-authored elements within an AI-assisted work — a human-written prompt that is itself creative, a human selection and arrangement of AI outputs — may be copyrightable, but only those elements.
The threshold for 'sufficient human authorship' is fact-specific and will be contested. Prompt engineering alone is unlikely to clear the bar in most cases.
European Union

The EU does not yet have a specific AI authorship rule. Under existing EU copyright law (InfoSoc Directive), copyright requires the work to be the author's 'own intellectual creation' — reflecting the author's personality and free creative choices. An output produced entirely by an AI without such choices cannot qualify.

The EU AI Act (2024) regulates high-risk AI systems but does not resolve copyright ownership. The European Commission has signalled that a sui generis right for AI-generated content (similar to the database right) is under consideration but not yet enacted. Enterprises operating in the EU should treat AI-only outputs as unprotected pending further legislative clarity.
United Kingdom

The UK has the most developed statutory position. Section 9(3) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 provides that for a 'computer-generated work' (defined as one with no human author), the author is 'the person by whom the arrangements necessary for the creation of the work are undertaken.' This provision — enacted in 1988, decades before modern AI — potentially allows copyright to vest in the operator of the AI system.

However, courts have not definitively ruled on whether modern large language models fall within this provision. The UK Intellectual Property Office's 2023 consultation proposed removing the provision, which would eliminate this protection. Enterprises with UK-centric operations should obtain specific legal advice before relying on s.9(3).
Training Data and Derivative Work Risk

A separate copyright risk arises from the training data used to build AI models. If a model was trained on copyrighted works without authorisation, outputs that reproduce or closely resemble those works may constitute infringement — regardless of who owns the output.
The Similarity Threshold

Courts apply a 'substantial similarity' test: is the output substantially similar to a copyrighted work in the training data? For code-generation models, literal code reproduction is the highest-risk scenario. For text models, verbatim quotation and close paraphrase both carry risk. For image models, style imitation at a sufficient level of specificity (mimicking a named artist's distinctive technique) has been held potentially infringing in ongoing US litigation.
Vendor Indemnification

Major AI vendors have responded to this risk with IP indemnification programmes. Microsoft Copilot Copyright Commitment, Google's generative AI indemnification, and Adobe's Content Credentials programme each offer some level of protection against third-party IP claims arising from outputs generated using their models. Enterprises should:
Read the indemnification scope precisely — most exclude outputs that reproduce training data verbatim or that were generated after the user disabled safety filters.
Confirm the indemnification covers your specific use case — commercial deployment, customer-facing use, and product embedding have different coverage levels.
Maintain records of which model version generated which output, since indemnification may not extend to outdated model versions.
Work-for-Hire and Employment Context

Even where copyright in an AI-assisted output vests in a human author, the employer's interest must be established separately. In most common-law jurisdictions, works created by an employee within the scope of their employment are 'works for hire' — copyright vests in the employer, not the employee. This analysis holds for AI-assisted work just as it does for traditional work.

The scope-of-employment test is the critical variable. An employee who uses a company AI tool to produce a work-related output during working hours creates a clear work-for-hire. An employee who uses the same AI tool to produce personal creative work on a personal device has a much weaker work-for-hire claim. Enterprises should:
Include explicit IP assignment language in employment contracts that covers AI-assisted outputs alongside traditionally authored works.
Define in the AI acceptable-use policy what constitutes 'work-related' use of company AI tools.
Establish a process for employees to disclose and assign rights to AI-assisted outputs of commercial significance.
Practical Copyright Policy for Enterprise AI Use
Scenario
Risk Level
Required Action
Internal documentation (non-commercial)
LOW
Standard document retention; note which outputs were AI-assisted
Customer-facing content (marketing, reports)
MEDIUM
Human review and edit to establish authorship; document human creative contributions
Product features built on AI-generated code
MEDIUM-HIGH
Legal review; check vendor indemnification scope; run similarity checks
AI-generated images for commercial use
HIGH
Use models with explicit commercial-use licences; verify training data provenance; obtain vendor indemnification
Competitive intelligence or research summaries
LOW-MEDIUM
Fact-check AI outputs; do not redistribute AI text verbatim
Training data for a fine-tuned model
HIGH
Separate legal analysis required for each data source
How Courts Have Ruled: Key Cases and Implications
Thaler v. Perlmutter (US, 2023)

Stephen Thaler applied to register copyright in an image generated entirely by his DABUS AI system, listing the AI as the sole author and himself as the work-for-hire employer. The US Copyright Office refused registration; the DC Circuit Court affirmed that human authorship is a 'bedrock requirement' of copyright. The court rejected the argument that feeding inputs into an AI and receiving an output constitutes authorship — the creative act must be the human's.
Enterprise implication:

Outputs produced by fully automated pipelines — where the AI selects, arranges, and produces the final work without human creative intervention — are not protectable in the US. If such outputs are commercially valuable, trade secret protection or contractual non-disclosure is the only available protection.
Getty Images v. Stability AI (UK/US, 2023)

Getty Images sued Stability AI, alleging that Stable Diffusion was trained on copyrighted images without licence. The case raised a distinct question from ownership — whether training an AI on copyrighted data without authorisation constitutes infringement of the training data, not just the output. As of 2024, the US and UK cases are ongoing.
Enterprise implication:

Enterprises that fine-tune foundation models on internal proprietary data must ensure the base model's training data licence permits such use. Enterprises that build products on third-party models should request training data provenance documentation from vendors.
Anderson v. Stability AI (US, 2023)

A class action by artists alleging that Stable Diffusion infringes copyright by generating images in the style of named artists. The district court dismissed several claims but allowed the direct infringement claim to proceed. The case will clarify whether AI outputs that closely mimic a named artist's style constitute derivative works.
Enterprise implication:

Prompts that explicitly request output in the style of a named living artist carry legal risk until this case resolves. The safest position is to avoid style-specific prompts for commercially deployed image outputs.
Recording and Auditing AI-Assisted Outputs
An enterprise cannot defend a copyright claim — or assert a copyright — without records of how an output was generated. A minimal output record should capture:
Field
Purpose
Retention
output_id
Link record to the deployed asset
Lifetime of asset
generating_model + version
Identifies indemnification coverage and training data epoch
Lifetime of asset
prompt_text
Establishes what human instruction produced the output
Lifetime of asset
human_creative_elements
Enumerates the human authorship claims for the output
Lifetime of asset
safety_filters_active
Confirms indemnification eligibility
Lifetime of asset
jurisdiction_of_use
Determines applicable copyright regime
Lifetime of asset
legal_review_completed
Documents that high-risk outputs were reviewed
7 years minimum

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class OutputAuditLog:
    """
    Append-only audit log for commercially significant AI outputs.
    Each entry corresponds to one GenAIOutputRecord.
    """
    entries: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    def record(
        self,
        output_record: "GenAIOutputRecord",
        use_case: str,
        is_commercial: bool,
        legal_review_completed: bool = False,
        legal_reviewer: str = "",
    ) -> dict:
        entry = {
            "output_id": output_record.output_id,
            "output_type": output_record.output_type,
            "model": output_record.generating_model,
            "date": str(output_record.date_generated),
            "jurisdiction": output_record.jurisdiction,
            "human_authorship_claimed": output_record.human_authorship_likely(),
            "risk_level": output_record.copyright_risk_level(),
            "ownership_assessment": output_record.ownership_assessment(),
            "use_case": use_case,
            "is_commercial": is_commercial,
            "vendor_indemnification": output_record.vendor_indemnification_applies,
            "safety_filters_active": output_record.safety_filters_active,
            "legal_review_completed": legal_review_completed,
            "legal_reviewer": legal_reviewer,
        }
        self.entries.append(entry)
        return entry

    def high_risk_without_review(self) -> list[dict]:
        return [
            e for e in self.entries
            if e["risk_level"] in ("HIGH", "MEDIUM") and
               e["is_commercial"] and
               not e["legal_review_completed"]
        ]

    def audit_summary(self) -> str:
        total = len(self.entries)
        commercial = sum(1 for e in self.entries if e["is_commercial"])
        high_risk = sum(1 for e in self.entries if e["risk_level"] == "HIGH")
        pending_review = len(self.high_risk_without_review())
        return (
            f"Output audit: {total} total | {commercial} commercial | "
            f"{high_risk} high-risk | {pending_review} pending legal review"
        )
Interaction With Open Source and Creative Commons Licences

When AI outputs are used in conjunction with open-source software or Creative Commons content, the licence terms of the incorporated material continue to apply independently of any copyright analysis on the AI output itself.
OSS Licence Propagation

A code-generation model may reproduce snippets of GPL-licenced code from its training data. If such a snippet is incorporated into a commercial product without satisfying the GPL's copyleft requirements (source disclosure, licence preservation), the enterprise faces an OSS compliance violation — distinct from the AI copyright question. This risk is highest for models trained on public code repositories without a filtering step that excludes strong copyleft code.
Mitigation:
Run AI-generated code through a licence compliance scanner (FOSSA, Black Duck, SCANOSS) before deployment. These tools detect known copyleft patterns even in modified form.
Model selection:
Some code-generation models have been specifically trained to avoid GPL and other strong copyleft code. Check vendor documentation for 'copyleft-free' or 'licence-filtered' training claims.
Creative Commons Content in Prompts

When a user provides CC-licenced content as context in a prompt (e.g., pasting a CC-BY article to summarise or transform), the AI output may contain substantial portions of the original work. The CC-BY licence requires attribution of the original author — an obligation that survives the AI transformation step and attaches to any published use of the output.
Licence obligations follow the content, not the transformation method. Using an AI to rephrase or summarise CC-licenced or OSS-licenced content does not discharge the original licence requirements.
Summary

Copyright ownership of GenAI-generated outputs depends on three factors: the degree of human creative contribution, the operating jurisdiction, and the risk profile of the specific use case. In the United States, outputs without sufficient human authorship receive no copyright protection. In the UK, the computer-generated work provision may apply but is under legislative review. In the EU, no AI-specific right yet exists.

For enterprises, the practical implications are: document human creative choices for commercially significant outputs; confirm vendor IP indemnification coverage before deploying AI outputs in products; include AI-assisted works in employment IP assignment clauses; and establish a tiered policy — using
OutputCopyrightPolicy.can_publish()
— that triggers legal review for high-risk scenarios without creating friction for low-risk internal use.
